brief.headline: Aluminium's latest LME official settled firmly higher - cash +$27.50 to $3,183.50 on 21-Jul with the cash-3M backwardation widening to +$13 and stock at 278,275 t, the lowest since 2022 - as Strait of Hormuz shipping stays largely stalled after a fresh run of tanker attacks, even while a 10-day ceasefire proposal keeps the near-term skew two-sided.
bullets: What moved: the latest published LME official (21-Jul) settled higher across the board - cash +$27.50 to $3,183.50, 3M +$23.00 to $3,170.50 - clearing the $3,160 pivot, while the cash-3M backwardation steepened to +$13.00 and LME stock fell another 1,500 t to 278,275 t.

Why it matters: Strait of Hormuz traffic is largely stalled after repeated tanker attacks (the crude tanker Al Bahyah was hit Tuesday, one seafarer killed), keeping roughly 9% of global primary supply at risk. The widening backwardation and record-low stocks show the physical market tightening, not just headline risk.

The two-sided catalyst: mediators have put a 10-day ceasefire proposal in front of Washington and Tehran. A durable truce would release the war-risk premium and reopen Gulf shipping; a collapse re-escalates. It is the swing variable for the next several sessions.

The offset and the watch: EGA's Al Taweelah alumina restart (full production since 10-Jul) and record Chinese exports (June 711 kt) cap the upside. Watch whether LME stock keeps drawing below 278,275 t, whether the backwardation keeps steepening on the next official, and the $3,230/$3,260 resistance stack.

notes: The alumina market remains the mirror image of metal: globally LOOSENING (EGA's 2.4 Mtpa restart ramping since 10-Jul, Guinea bauxite oversupply, Chinese port stocks building; SMM Alumina Index down to ¥2,730.17/t, −¥12.8 w/w) — ex-China consolidation has stepped down toward the $300–330 band (FOB Australia now $328, from ~$338).

The regional wrinkle has widened further overnight: a 9th consecutive night of US strikes on Iran, a second consecutive Iranian strike on Kuwait's Shuaiba desalination/power complex, and Strait transits down to as few as ~3 vessels/24h all re-price the war-risk cover embedded in the ~60% of Gulf alumina imports that transit Hormuz. SMM separately flags ex-China metallurgical-grade output down ~6% y/y in June.

Guinea's pending export-quota policy (a considered cap near 150 Mt for 2026, vs 178 Mt shipped in 2025, targeting >$100/dmt bauxite) remains undecreed as of end-June and is the one clear bullish catalyst on the commodity leg; record Australian and Metro Mining shipments underline how long the seaborne bauxite market currently is.

headlines.title: The US-Iran conflict entered an 11th night of strikes and Iran hit another tanker in the Strait of Hormuz early Tuesday, while Yemen's Houthis declared a maritime embargo against Saudi Arabia - a potential new front. The US is moving to blockade Iranian ports as Washington seeks control of the strait. Hormuz traffic is largely stalled.

Regional mediators presented Washington and Tehran with a proposal for a 10-day ceasefire. Large divisions remain and the June-14 memorandum of understanding already collapsed once in July, but a durable truce would release the war-risk premium and reopen Gulf shipping - the key two-sided catalyst for the flat price.

EGA's Al Taweelah refinery has been at full alumina production since 10-Jul, targeting 50% of capacity within days and full capability by end-2026. At 2.4 Mt in 2025 it supplies close to half of EGA's alumina need - the only material supply-side relief currently visible and a key reason the flat price is ranging rather than spiking.

LME stock fell to 279,775 t on the 20-Jul official, the lowest since 2022 and roughly 45% below the 509,250 t start-of-year level, with SHFE stockpiles also declining. Cash-3M widened to a +$8.50 backwardation - prompt tightness, not surplus.

Norsk Hydro released Q2 2026 results this morning (22-Jul, 07:00 CEST) - the first European producer P&L covering a full quarter of the disruption regime and the cleanest public read on European smelter power costs and regional premium pass-through. Guidance into the quarter had pointed to adjusted EBITDA around NOK 8.7-9.0bn.

earnings.note: Revenue $3.97bn (+24% q/q, highest in nearly a decade); adj. EPS $2.12 (MISS vs ~$2.31 consensus); adj. net income $562m; adj. EBITDA $901m (from $595m in Q1); Aluminum segment RECORD EBITDA $1.073bn at 32.3% margin. First producer P&L under the Hormuz-disruption regime: record aluminium margins and…

Q2 2026 results released this morning (22-Jul, 07:00 CEST). Guidance into the quarter pointed to adjusted EBITDA ~NOK 8.7-9.0bn, with ~67% of Q2 primary output hedged near $3,000/t and realised premiums guided at ~$530-580/t. Final reported figures were still being digested at this ~06:05 London ref…
Adj. EPS $1.63 (beat $1.56); revenue $649m; net income $327m (incl. $288m Hawesville sale gain). Higher LME + strong US Midwest premiums; Q2 adj. EBITDA guided $315–335m.

Net profit $200.3m (+316% YoY); EPS 53 fils; sales −17% on Gulf supply-chain disruption. LME averaged $3,195/t (+22% YoY) drove profit surge; volumes hit by regional conflict; ~19% capacity in controlled shutdown — restart path improves post-truce.
Net profit SAR 1.63bn (~$436m, +5.5% YoY); revenue SAR 8.79bn (+3.2%); EBITDA $964m. Strong pricing across commodities; partly offset by lower phosphate volumes; 2026 capex set >$4bn.

H1 copper-equivalent output +3% y/y; aluminium division improved; strong bauxite recovery; FY aluminium guidance UNCHANGED at 3.25–3.45 Mt; Pilbara Q2 shipments 89 Mt (+5%). First upstream print of the Q2 season (this morning): Western volumes are intact — the Gulf disruption is expressing itself in…
Hillside Al ~538kt (9M, FY26 guide 720kt); Brazil Alumina record 1,060kt (9M); Worsley steady. Alumina sales −15% q/q on availability/timing; annual guidance intact despite cyclone gas disruption.

Hindalco PAT ₹5,283cr (+66% YoY); India Al upstream EBITDA margin ~46%; Novelis FY26 adj. EBITDA $1.6bn (−9%). Record India operations; Novelis pressured by Oswego outage (~$104m) and tariffs (~$143m).
Net profit guided RMB 5.30–5.59bn (+50–58% YoY). Strong on higher Al prices; China output near self-imposed 45 Mt capacity cap.
Underlying profit $6.2bn (+>20%); underlying EBITDA $15.5bn (+25%, 58% margin); interim div 73c. Copper-led strength; broad base-metals read-across positive for the complex.
FY25 results; copper & aluminium average prices +9% YoY; zinc metal +4%. Resilient structural demand for Cu/Al cited; broadly constructive sector tone.
FY25 net loss $455m (vs $803m profit in 2024) on higher costs & lower output. Analysts expect stronger H1 2026 on higher prices; Asia pivot amid fractured trade flows.

Guides H1-26 net profit ¥1.8–1.95bn, +154.4–175.6% YoY. Credits industry-chain cost reduction, aluminium prices fluctuating at highs, high-value-added deep-processing mix and energy-saving retrofits — the first sizeable China H1 pre-announcement of the season and a positive read-across for Chinese p…

catalysts.text: Norsk Hydro Q2 2026 results (released this morning; webcast 08:30 CEST) - first European producer P&L covering a full quarter of the disruption regime; read on European smelter power costs and regional premium pass-through
Next LME official ring - the first settled print able to reflect the 11th-night strikes and Tuesday's tanker attack; watch whether the cash-3M backwardation steepens beyond +$8.50
LME warehouse stock report - whether draws continue below 280,100 t, the lowest level since 2022
EGA Al Taweelah ramp from 50% of capacity toward full capability by end-2026 - the principal supply-side offset in the current balance
Q4-2026 MJP CIF Japan premium negotiations open against the decade-high Q3 settlement of $395/t

The 10-day ceasefire proposal in front of Washington and Tehran - traction would reopen Gulf shipping and release the war premium; collapse would re-escalate. The single biggest two-sided swing factor

risks.text: Hormuz / Gulf escalation: an 11th night of US strikes, a fresh tanker attack on 21-Jul, and a Houthi maritime-embargo threat against Saudi Arabia keep roughly 9% of global primary aluminium supply at risk with strait traffic largely stalled; a widening into ge…
Inventory floor: LME stock at 279,775 t is the lowest since 2022 and roughly -45% YTD with SHFE also drawing - there is no inventory buffer to absorb a fresh supply interruption
Energy cost squeeze: Brent above $90/bbl with EU gas following on a lag compresses marginal-producer margins in Europe and Asia and biases capacity toward curtailment rather than restart
Al Taweelah ramp disappoints or is interrupted - the alumina relief is the principal offset holding the flat price down and it is a ramp toward end-2026, not delivered tonnage
Demand destruction: sustained elevated prices plus the 50% Section 232 rate on US imports encourage substitution and scrap displacement, particularly in packaging and construction

Two-sided diplomacy: a durable 10-day ceasefire would reopen Gulf shipping and collapse the war-risk premium sharply (downside to price); a collapse like the June MoU would re-escalate. A Section 232 rate change would likewise reprice the US premium complex qu…

caveats: Report refreshed Wed 22-Jul-2026 (evening London). The benchmark table and lme_series carry the latest PUBLISHED LME official, the 21-Jul settlement (cash $3,183.50 / 3M $3,170.50 / stock 278,275 t) from Westmetall; the 22-Jul official posts on Westmetall with a session lag. Official settlements are the reference for the benchmark table.

MIXED SOURCE BASIS ON THE BOARD: the aluminium level on the metals board is set to the LME 21-Jul 3-month official (Westmetall) for a clean, consistent basis; copper is the latest LME 3M level (~$13,800/t); nickel, zinc and lead are the latest publicly reported prints (Trading Economics, dated 21-Jul). Official and traded prices are different reference points and will not tie out exactly.

GEOPOLITICAL DEVELOPMENTS ARE FAST-MOVING and largely post-date any settled LME official. The tanker attacks, the Houthi embargo threat and the 10-day ceasefire proposal continue to evolve; treat the flat price as lagging the news flow, and note the ceasefire proposal makes the near-term skew two-sided.

PREMIUM ASSESSMENTS ARE PERIODIC, NOT DAILY: MJP settles quarterly, Rotterdam duty-unpaid and US Midwest are assessed by Platts/Fastmarkets on their own schedules. The figures shown are the latest PUBLIC prints (MJP Q3 settled $395/t on 06-Jul). They are not daily marks.

ALL CONTENT IS PUBLIC MARKET INFORMATION for situational awareness only. No confidential, internal, company-specific or position data is included, and nothing here is trading, hedging or investment advice. Elliott Wave analysis is technical context, not a forecast or recommendation.
